one_line_takeaway: PonziGuard detects Ethereum Ponzi contracts by driving them in an instrumented, taint-tracking EVM to build a runtime behavior graph classified by a GAT-GNN, reaching 97.5% F1 and catching transaction-free 0-day scams that static- and transaction-based detectors miss.
key_finding: On the XBlock ground-truth dataset (6,498 contracts, 314 Ponzi), PonziGuard reaches 96.9% precision / 98.2% recall / 97.5% F1, beating all five baselines; the closest, SourceP, trails at 92.4% F1 and the transaction-based TxML is far behind at 66.4% F1. Ablations show both flow types matter (control-flow-only drops F1 by 12.2 pts, data-flow-only by 19.3 pts, neither by 35.3 pts) and that graph pruning and subgraph joining are both necessary (removing joining drops precision/recall/F1 by 5.9/10.2/8.0 pts). Doc2Vec node embeddings beat one-hot (F1 96.4% vs 85.2%) and are robust to paraphrasing of the opcode descriptions, indicating the gain comes from semantics not surface text. Applied to ~14M Mainnet blocks it found 805 Ponzi contracts (100% TP on a 50-contract manual sample) accounting for 281,700 Ether inflow (~$500M) over 615,483 transactions, and it detected 15 0-day Ponzi contracts among 10,000 contracts deployed in March 2024, most with only their creation transaction.
core_contribution: PonziGuard, a detection approach for Ethereum Ponzi-scheme smart contracts that does NOT rely on static opcode statistics or on-chain transaction history. Its central novel artifact is the Contract Runtime Behavior Graph (CRBG): the authors generate synthetic transaction sequences (guided by static analysis) to drive the contract inside an instrumented EVM, run dynamic taint analysis to capture runtime control-flow and data-flow, build a graph from those runtime traces, and then formulate Ponzi detection as a GNN graph-classification task. This is an extended version of the authors' ICSE 2024 conference paper, adding a static-analysis-guided transaction generator, graph pruning/subgraph-joining, a larger comparison, interpretability and ablation studies, and a 0-day detection experiment. Because CRBG is derived from driven execution rather than real transactions, PonziGuard can flag 0-day Ponzi contracts that have no transaction history.
threat_model: Adversaries deploy smart contracts on Ethereum that operate as Ponzi/investment scams: they lure victims with promises of high returns, pay early investors out of later investors' funds, and eventually collapse or self-destruct while the operator absconds with the funds. PonziGuard is a DEFENSIVE tool for the auditor/platform side: given a contract (and its source, which slither analyzes), it decides Ponzi vs. benign, ideally before any victim transacts (0-day). The paper's Section 2.2 criteria explicitly bound the target class (must have both investment and reward logic, funds from a multitude of investors, and all investors promised rewards), deliberately excluding gambling/puzzle contracts that superficially resemble Ponzi schemes. The paper does not study an adaptive attacker who tries to evade CRBG, so robustness to evasion is out of scope.
limitations: 1) The approach requires driving the contract and re-executing transactions in an instrumented EVM, adding measured overhead of up to ~30.2% (ground-truth, 1,000 tx) and 25.5% (500k-block replay); the full-Mainnet historical replay is described by the authors as time-consuming and only a 'preliminary experiment' synced to ~14M blocks (Jan 2022), not exhaustive. 2) The static-analysis stage and the 0-day experiment rely on contract SOURCE CODE (slither, Etherscan source, Remix inspection), so coverage of source-less bytecode-only contracts is not demonstrated. 3) Transaction sequence generation is heuristic/random (keyword lists, random function choice, increasing Ether), so some Ponzi behaviors may never be triggered — reflected in <100% recall. 4) Real-world 'ground truth' relies on manual confirmation (50-contract sample; 15 0-day contracts manually confirmed), not an independent labeled set. 5) No adaptive/evasion adversary is evaluated. 6) The $500M figure is a current-exchange-rate valuation of aggregate Ether inflow, not confirmed victim loss.

datasets_used: XBlock ground-truth Ponzi dataset (Zheng et al.): 6,498 smart contracts, 314 labeled Ponzi, block heights 0 to 7,500,000, manually cross-checked
Ethereum Mainnet historical blocks: ~14,000,000 blocks synced up to January 2022, all transactions re-executed from genesis
10,000 smart contracts recently deployed on Ethereum in March 2024 (0-day detection set)
Ethereum Yellow Paper opcode descriptions (source text for Doc2Vec node embeddings)
quantitative_results: PonziGuard on ground-truth: precision 96.9%, recall 98.2%, F1 97.5%
Real-world: 805 Ponzi contracts found on Mainnet; 497 have source on Etherscan; 50-contract manual sample = 100% true positive rate
Financial impact: 281,700 Ether over 615,483 transactions inflow (~$500M at current rate); peak Feb-2018 = 117,953 Ether (~$108M at the time); average Ponzi contract lifetime ~7.1 months
0-day: 15 Ponzi contracts confirmed among 10,000 March-2024 contracts, most with only a creation transaction
Ablation - no graph pruning: avg nodes/edges +~30%, lower P/R/F1; no subgraph joining: nodes cut to ~1/5, P/R/F1 drop 5.9/10.2/8.0 pts
Ablation - flow edges vs full (DF+CF): CF-only drops 9.3/15/12.2 pts; DF-only drops 13.5/24.2/19.3 pts; NE (neither) drops 26.4/42.1/35.3 pts
Node embeddings (Table 4): one-hot MOE 88.5/82.1/85.2; enhanced Doc2Vec MEE 96.4/96.4/96.4; paraphrase Variant1 96.3/92.9/94.5; Variant2 96.2/89.3/92.6
Overhead: instrumented EVM max ~30.2% on 1,000 transactions; modified Geth 25.5% on 500,000-block replay
baselines_compared: OpML (Chen et al., XGBoost on opcode frequency): 89.0% / 77.8% / 83.0%
